1: "Now the famine was still severe in the land" — the second journey is necessitated by the same prolonged famine interpreted by Joseph in Genesis 41 (seven years of famine following seven years of plenty). The time elapsed since the first journey (Genesis 42) is implied by Genesis 43:2: they have eaten all the grain they brought back. The famine's ongoing severity — not a brief shortage but a multi-year regional crisis — is the pressure that forces Jacob's hand on the Benjamin question. The ANE record from Egypt's Middle Kingdom period includes inscriptions referencing Canaanite peoples coming to Egypt during famine years, consistent with the narrative's geopolitical setting.
2: "Their father said to them, 'Go back and buy us a little more food'" — Jacob's request is framed with a diminutive ("a little more food," šiḇrû-lānû meʿaṭ-ʾōkhel), as if the scale of the request minimizes the problem it creates — namely, that a return to Egypt requires Benjamin's presence. Jacob has been refusing to acknowledge the Benjamin condition since the brothers returned from their first journey; hunger has made the refusal untenable. "A little more food" is both literally true (they need food) and euphemistic (they need to face a crisis they have been avoiding).
3: "The man warned us solemnly, 'You will not see my face again unless your brother is with you'" — Judah's reminder to Jacob is the third repetition of Joseph's demand (first in Genesis 42:20, then recalled in Genesis 42:33-34, now cited again here). The formula "you will not see my face" (lōʾ tirʾû pānāy) is the standard ANE expression for being denied access to an official or king — the inverse of "finding favor in the eyes of." Judah quotes the condition as irrevocable and non-negotiable, which it is: Egypt controls the food supply, and the Egyptian official controls access to it.
4: "If you will send our brother along with us, we will go down and buy food for you" — the conditional structure is blunt: send Benjamin, or we cannot go. Judah speaks for the brothers as a group; the shift of Judah to spokesperson (he is not the firstborn, but Reuben has already failed to secure Jacob's consent in Genesis 42:37-38) reflects the leadership dynamic that will culminate in Judah's climactic speech in Genesis 44. Judah has become the family's political voice in the crisis situations that matter most.
5: "But if you will not send him, we will not go down, because the man said to us, 'You will not see my face unless your brother is with you'" — the ultimatum is restated: the brothers cannot return to Egypt without Benjamin, and the brothers will not go without a clear decision from Jacob. The standoff has lasted long enough for the first supply of grain to run out entirely. Hunger is the decisive argument that no parental reluctance can ultimately overcome.
6: "Why did you bring this trouble on me by telling the man you had another brother?" — Jacob's complaint uses the same verb (harēʿōtem) as in Genesis 34:30 (after the Shechem massacre: "you have brought trouble on me"). His grievance is that the brothers volunteered information about Benjamin that was not required — though in Genesis 42:13-16, the Egyptian official directly questioned them about their family, making the disclosure responsive rather than spontaneous. Jacob is looking for someone to blame; the brothers' reply (Genesis 43:7) accurately points out that the official asked the questions.
7: "The man questioned us closely about ourselves and our family" — the brothers' account of Joseph's interrogation is accurate: in Genesis 42:7-20, Joseph questioned them about their origins, family, and father. The questions were not casual; they were specifically designed to elicit information about Jacob and Benjamin — information Joseph needed to orchestrate the subsequent events. The brothers could not have anticipated that the questions were personal rather than official; from their perspective, they answered a government official's routine immigration inquiry.
8: "Then Judah said to Israel his father, 'Send the boy along with me and we will go at once, so that we and you and our children may live and not die'" — Judah's appeal is structured around survival: "we and you and our children may live and not die" encompasses the entire household across three generations. The urgency ("go at once," nāqûmāh wĕnēlēkhāh) is reinforced by the stark alternative. Judah addresses Jacob using both names — "Israel his father" — the covenant name that carries the weight of the family's divine calling, perhaps deliberately invoking Jacob's identity as the covenant recipient to motivate action.
9: "I myself will guarantee his safety; you can hold me personally responsible for him" — Judah's personal surety (ʾānōkhî ʾeʿerĕḇennû, I will be surety for him) is a legal instrument: in ANE commercial law, surety (ʿārēḇ) meant assuming personal liability for another's debt or obligation. Reuben had offered his two sons as collateral (Genesis 42:37), which was grotesque — pledging children for a father's peace of mind. Judah offers himself, which is both more proportionate and more genuinely personal. The commitment "all my life" (kol-hayyāmîm) is lifelong liability, not a limited guarantee. This pledge will be tested and redeemed in ch.Genesis 44:33.
10: "If we had not delayed, we could have gone and returned twice" — Judah's practical calculation underlines the cost of Jacob's hesitation. The journey from Canaan to Egypt and back was roughly 250-300 miles each direction, taking approximately two weeks each way; a full round trip was a month or more. Two such round trips could have been completed in the time lost to the stalemate. The point is not recrimination but urgency: delay costs lives, and further delay will cost more.
11: "Put some of the best products of the land in your bags and take them down to the man as a gift — a little balm and a little honey, some spices and myrrh, some pistachio nuts and almonds" — the gift list is an exact echo of Genesis 37:25, where the Ishmaelite traders heading to Egypt carried "spices, balm and myrrh" — the same products that were in the caravan that purchased Joseph and took him to Egypt. Jacob is now sending the brothers to Egypt carrying the same Canaanite exports that Joseph was taken south with. The echo is most likely intentional narrative art: the commerce that separated the family is now embedded in the gift that attempts to restore it.
12: "Take double the amount of silver with you, for you must return the silver that was put back into the mouths of your sacks. Perhaps it was a mistake" — double silver (the original payment returned to them plus a new payment) reflects proper ANE commercial ethics: if you receive property you did not intend to take, you return it and pay for any benefit derived. "Perhaps it was a mistake" (ûlûlay mišgeh hûʾ) is Jacob's most generous interpretation of the silver mystery; he does not accuse the Egyptian official of deliberate entrapment but offers the most favorable reading, which is also the safest diplomatic approach.
13: "Take your brother also and go back to the man at once" — Jacob's consent is delivered in two short commands: take the gifts, take Benjamin. The brevity after the extended deliberation of Genesis 43:1-12 suggests resignation rather than enthusiasm; Jacob has been maneuvered into a decision by hunger and Judah's persuasion. His prayer in Genesis 43:14 is the internal state behind the external command: he is complying while entreating God's mercy on the outcome.
14: "May God Almighty grant you mercy before the man so that he will let your other brother and Benjamin come back with you" — Jacob invokes ʾEl-Šaddai (God Almighty), the covenant name used in critical moments of the patriarchal narrative (Genesis 17:1; Genesis 28:3; Genesis 35:11). The prayer is structurally parallel to Esther 4:16 ("If I perish, I perish") and represents the resigned faith of someone who has done what he can and commits the outcome to God. "Your other brother" (Simeon, still held in Egypt since Genesis 42:24) is mentioned before Benjamin — Jacob remembers both at-risk sons. The phrase "if I am bereaved, I am bereaved" (waʾănî kaʾăšer šākhaltî šākhāltî) is the acceptance of worst-case loss while still acting; it is not despair but resigned obedience.
15: "They hurried down to Egypt and presented themselves to Joseph" — the journey is summarized without detail; the narrator's interest is in the Egyptian encounter. "Presented themselves" (wayyaʿamĕḏû lip̄nê yôsēp̄, they stood before Joseph's face) uses the court presentation language; the brothers are reporting to an official before whom they must account for their presence and purpose. The previous journey's tension (accusation, Simeon's imprisonment, the silver mystery) looms over this presentation.
16: "When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the steward of his house, 'Slaughter an animal and prepare dinner; they are to eat with me at noon'" — Joseph's recognition of Benjamin triggers an immediate, generous response: a noon feast. Noon (haṣṣohŏrāyim, "the two-hours" or midday) was the main meal of the Egyptian day — a substantial invitation, not a light reception. The slaughter of an animal for the feast signals a significant hospitality occasion, beyond ordinary provisioning. Joseph's response is emotional relief (seeing his full brother is alive and present) translated into ANE hospitality action.
17: "The man did as Joseph told him and took the men to Joseph's house" — the steward executes Joseph's instructions without delay; the household's efficient compliance with Joseph's directives reflects the organizational portrait of Genesis 41-42 (Joseph as master administrator). Being taken to Joseph's private house rather than a public reception area is both an honor and an anxious development for the brothers, who do not yet understand that the invitation is benevolent.
18: "Now the men were frightened when they were taken to his house. They thought, 'We were brought here because of the silver that was put back in our sacks the first time. He wants to attack us and overpower us and seize us as slaves and take our donkeys'" — the brothers' fear is legally grounded. In ANE law, finding another's property in your possession could constitute evidence of theft; theft could result in debt-slavery. Their specific fear — "seize us as slaves" — reflects the understood legal consequence. The irony is rich: they sold their brother into slavery, and now they fear being enslaved. Their mental reconstruction of what will happen to them is a form of projected guilt operating without their conscious recognition.
19: "So they went up to Joseph's steward and spoke to him at the entrance to the house" — the brothers address the steward rather than waiting to be received — a proactive attempt to explain their situation before any accusation is made. Addressing a household steward at the entrance was the appropriate protocol when seeking to present oneself to a superior's household; the brothers are following diplomatic form while in considerable anxiety.
20: "We beg your pardon, our lord, we came down here the first time to buy food" — the self-presentation begins with the most basic legitimating fact: the purpose of the original visit was commercial, not criminal. The honorific "our lord" (bî ʾăḏōnî, literally "in me, my lord," a standard deference opener) is the appropriate address for a senior household official. The brothers are trying to establish their good faith before the silver issue is raised.
21: "But at the place where we stopped for the night we opened our sacks and each of us found his silver — the exact weight — in the mouth of his sack" — the brothers' account of the silver discovery is precise: "the exact weight" (bĕmišqālô, by its weight) indicates they verified it was the same silver they had paid, not a random amount. The exactness of the returned silver is itself puzzling — if it had been an accident, one might expect an inexact amount; the precision suggests deliberate replacement. The brothers are presenting this as an inexplicable event, not a concealed crime.
22: "We don't know who put our silver in our sacks" — the disclaimer of knowledge is both accurate and legally protective: in ANE legal reasoning, possession of stolen property with demonstrated ignorance of its origin could mitigate culpability. The brothers have brought additional silver for the new purchase and are offering to return the old silver — a complete good-faith disclosure that argues against criminal intent.
23: "Your God, the God of your father, has given you treasure in your sacks; I received your payment" — the Egyptian steward speaks a theological claim that identifies YHWH as the source of the mysterious silver. The steward could not have known this independently; Joseph has clearly briefed his household manager on what to say and how to handle this situation. Joseph's mercy is being enacted through his steward's words: the silver mystery that terrified the brothers is reframed as divine gift. Simeon is brought out immediately — the missing brother is restored without negotiation, as Joseph had always intended to return him.
24: "The steward took the men into Joseph's house, gave them water to wash their feet and provided fodder for their donkeys" — foot-washing on arrival was the standard ANE hospitality gesture for receiving guests (cf. Abraham and Lot's foot-washing offers in 18:4 and Genesis 19:2; the washing of disciples' feet in John 13:14 situates itself within this hospitality tradition). Fodder for the donkeys completes the dual hospitality: host provides for both the human guests and their animals. These acts signal that the brothers are guests, not prisoners — a reassurance enacted through ANE hospitality protocol before any word is spoken.
25: "They prepared their gifts for Joseph's arrival at noon, because they had heard that they were to eat there" — the brothers arrange their tribute gift in preparation for the noon meal; the presentation of gifts before a superior was a formal protocol in ANE court settings. Their preparation also indicates they have accepted the unexpected hospitality as genuine — the steward's reassurance (Genesis 43:23) has sufficiently calmed them to proceed with the planned diplomatic presentation.
26: "When Joseph came home, they presented to him the gifts they had brought inside, and they bowed down before him to the ground" — the prostration before Joseph fulfills the dream of Genesis 37:7-9 (the brothers' sheaves bowing to Joseph's). The brothers' involuntary fulfillment of the dream they once mocked — they had asked "will you actually reign over us? Will you actually rule us?" (Genesis 37:8) — is being enacted without their knowledge or intention. Joseph, who remembers the dreams (Genesis 42:9: "he remembered his dreams about them"), experiences this fulfillment knowingly.
27: "He asked them how they were, and then he said, 'How is your aged father you told me about? Is he still living?'" — Joseph's inquiry after Jacob is the first personal question he asks — disguised as official curiosity ("the one you told me about") but revealing his personal need to know. The phrase "aged father" (ʾăḇîkhem haqqāzên) — the aging father whose death would devastate the family — is both a diplomatic inquiry and an expression of genuine filial anxiety. Jacob's age and fragility have been a recurring motif in the brothers' own narrative (Genesis 42:38; Genesis 44:20, 22, 29-31).
28: "They replied, 'Your servant our father is still alive and well.' And they bowed down, prostrating themselves before him" — the double prostration in this verse (they bowed after Genesis 43:26 and bow again here) reflects the formal protocol of multiple honor gestures in a sustained royal audience. The brothers' identification of Jacob as "your servant our father" applies the vassal title to the absent patriarch — a formal acknowledgment of the Egyptian official's superiority over the entire family, including the patriarch.
29: "As he looked about and saw his brother Benjamin, his own mother's son, he asked, 'Is this your youngest brother, the one you told me about?' And he said, 'God be gracious to you, my son'" — Benjamin is identified three ways: by sight (Joseph recognizes him), by relationship (his own mother's son — both are sons of Rachel), and by narrative reference ("the one you told me about"). Joseph's blessing — "God be gracious to you, my son" (ʾĕlōhîm yāḥonnĕkhā bĕnî) — is remarkable: he uses the paternal address "my son" (bĕnî) to a brother he has not seen since Benjamin was a small child (Joseph was 17 when sold; Benjamin was younger). The blessing is personal, not official.
30: "Deeply moved at the sight of his brother, Joseph hurried out and looked for a place to weep. He went into his private room and wept there" — the phrase "deeply moved" translates wayyikhmerû raḥămāyw (his compassion/womb burned), a visceral expression of overwhelming emotion. The word raḥămîm (compassion, literally "womb") is used for the most intense emotional bonds; it shares a root with the word for womb and implies the deep, unchosen emotional response of a parent or close kin. Joseph's self-removal to a private room (haḥaḏer) to weep maintains his Egyptian official persona before the brothers while allowing him to experience the full force of his emotion privately.
31: "After washing his face, he came out and, controlling himself, said, 'Serve the food'" — the face-washing is a preparation for re-presentation: in the ANE, weeping was a public activity, and wet eyes would be immediately visible. Joseph washes and controls himself (wayyi ʾěḏar ʿaṣmô, he restrained himself) before returning — a deliberate act of emotional management consistent with his role as an official conducting a covert family test. The abrupt "serve the food" is a court command that re-establishes the official frame.
32: "They served him by himself, the brothers by themselves, and the Egyptians who ate with him by themselves, because Egyptians could not eat with Hebrews, for that is detestable to Egyptians" — the three-table arrangement reflects historically documented Egyptian food-purity practices. Egyptian texts describe avoidance of eating with foreigners; some cults prohibited eating with people of other categories. The specific term "detestable to Egyptians" (tôʿēḇāh, the same word used for abominations in the Holiness Code) emphasizes the cultural/ritual character of the separation. Joseph's unusual intermediate status — eating alone, neither with Egyptians nor with Hebrews — reflects his liminal identity: he is both but not fully either in this scene.
33: "The men had been seated before him in the order of their ages, from the firstborn to the youngest; and they looked at each other in astonishment" — the seating in precise birth order is a detail that cannot be ascribed to coincidence or Egyptian protocol; it signals to the careful reader that the Egyptian official has specific, personal knowledge of this family. The brothers' astonishment (wayyitmahhû, they were astonished/dumbfounded) is their second moment of inexplicable recognition that something unusual is occurring — the first was the silver returned to their sacks. Both events accumulate evidence that this official knows them more intimately than any official should.
34: "Benjamin's portion was five times as much as anyone else's" — the five-fold portion given to Benjamin is a test: will the other brothers react with jealousy to a favored younger brother being given more than his share? The special treatment of Benjamin mirrors the special treatment of Joseph (Genesis 37:3-4, the long robe) that provoked the brothers to hatred. The brothers' response — "they feasted and drank freely with him" — indicates they pass the test without visible resentment. The journey from Genesis 37 to Genesis 43 has changed something in the brothers, though they are not yet aware of how fully they will be tested in the next chapter.
